{{ill|seven great temples founded by Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q11610998}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q339859]] Wikidata name: Shitennō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Ōsaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Arahakasan Shitennō-ji | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Shitennoji03s3200.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = {{nihongo||中心伽藍|[[shichidō garan|Chushin garan]]}} {{Infobox mapframe|type=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=220}} | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|39|14.04|N|135|30|59.22|E|region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | location = 1-1-18 Shitennō-ji | municipality = [[:en:Tennōji-ku, Osaka|Tennōji-ku]], Osaka | prefecture = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka-fu]] | religious_affiliation = [[Wa-shū]] | deity = [[Kannon]] (Avalokiteśvara) | country = Japan | functional_status = | general_contractor = [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] | established=593 | website = {{url|shitennoji.or.jp}} | founded_by = [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] | year_completed = 1963 (reconstruction) }}
'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
<gallery widths="240" heights="200"> Shitennô-ji Model.jpg|Model of the temple complex at the time of its construction, at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]]. Shitennō-ji main hall and pagoda.png|Tinted photo of the larger main hall and pagoda around 1880, prior to the downscaled modern reconstruction. Taken by [[:en:Kusakabe Kimbei|Kusakabe Kimbei]]. 1934 Typhoon Muroto damage at Shitenno-ji.jpg|The rubble of the five-tiered pagoda after destruction by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|1934 Muroto typhoon]]. </gallery>
== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The temple Prince Shōtoku built to honor them had four institutions, each to help the Japanese attain a higher level of civilization. This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. Three of the four sections are known to have existed inside the temple in Kamakura period{{Citation needed|date=March 2009}}.
The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. Surrounding this central complex are the {{nihongo|Great South Gate||Nandaimon}}, and a {{nihongo|Great East Gate||Higashi-no-ō'mon}}. To the west is the {{nihongo|Great West Gate||Nishi-no-ō'mon}}, also known as {{nihongo||極楽門|Gokuraku-mon}}. Further to the west is a stone [[torii]], which is imagined to be the Eastern Gate to [[:en:Sukhavati|Sukhavati]], the Pure Land of the West.
In the Kameido hall is a 7th-century turtle-shaped stonework that was used for state rituals with water.<ref name="mainichi">{{cite news |newspaper=Mainichi Daily News |title=Turtle-shaped stonework at Osaka temple dates to 7th century: study |date=April 27, 2019 |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124075101/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-date=November 24, 2020}}</ref> These are 2 turtle-shaped objects in opposite direction.<ref name="mainichi"/> The stone tank and upper turtle's base were carved from single pieces of Tatsuyama stone. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]

'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
<gallery widths="240" heights="200"> Shitennô-ji Model.jpg|Model of the temple complex at the time of its construction, at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]]. Shitennō-ji main hall and pagoda.png|Tinted photo of the larger main hall and pagoda around 1880, prior to the downscaled modern reconstruction. Taken by [[:en:Kusakabe Kimbei|Kusakabe Kimbei]]. 1934 Typhoon Muroto damage at Shitenno-ji.jpg|The rubble of the five-tiered pagoda after destruction by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|1934 Muroto typhoon]]. </gallery>
== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The temple Prince Shōtoku built to honor them had four institutions, each to help the Japanese attain a higher level of civilization. This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. Three of the four sections are known to have existed inside the temple in Kamakura period{{Citation needed|date=March 2009}}.
The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. Surrounding this central complex are the {{nihongo|Great South Gate||Nandaimon}}, and a {{nihongo|Great East Gate||Higashi-no-ō'mon}}. To the west is the {{nihongo|Great West Gate||Nishi-no-ō'mon}}, also known as {{nihongo||極楽門|Gokuraku-mon}}. Further to the west is a stone [[torii]], which is imagined to be the Eastern Gate to [[:en:Sukhavati|Sukhavati]], the Pure Land of the West.
In the Kameido hall is a 7th-century turtle-shaped stonework that was used for state rituals with water.<ref name="mainichi">{{cite news |newspaper=Mainichi Daily News |title=Turtle-shaped stonework at Osaka temple dates to 7th century: study |date=April 27, 2019 |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124075101/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-date=November 24, 2020}}</ref> These are 2 turtle-shaped objects in opposite direction.<ref name="mainichi"/> The stone tank and upper turtle's base were carved from single pieces of Tatsuyama stone. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="5"> File:Shitennoji - pagoda.jpg|Shitennō-ji [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] File:Shitennoji07s3200.jpg|Kondō File:Shitennoji01s3200.jpg|The Great West Gate (Gokuraku-mon) File:Shitennoji daimon1.jpg|The Great East Gate File:Shitennoji daimon.jpg|The Great South Gate ({{transliteration|ja|Nandai-mon}}) File:Shitennoji4.jpg|Northern belfry File:Shitennoji-taishi-indoushoudo.jpg|Southern belfry File:Shitennoji-torii.jpg|Stone torii File:Shitennoji06s3200.jpg|Courtyard File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou1.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou2.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio1.jpg|Daimon 1 File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio2.jpg|Daimon 2 File:Shitennoji-KoboDaishi2 Osaka 20101013.jpg|Statuettes of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] </gallery>

'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>

The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
<gallery widths="240" heights="200"> Shitennô-ji Model.jpg|Model of the temple complex at the time of its construction, at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]]. Shitennō-ji main hall and pagoda.png|Tinted photo of the larger main hall and pagoda around 1880, prior to the downscaled modern reconstruction. Taken by [[:en:Kusakabe Kimbei|Kusakabe Kimbei]]. 1934 Typhoon Muroto damage at Shitenno-ji.jpg|The rubble of the five-tiered pagoda after destruction by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|1934 Muroto typhoon]]. </gallery>
== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. {{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|New Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q11504057}}; {{ill|Q11078809|WD=Q11078809}}; {{ill|Q11274431|WD=Q11274431}}; {{ill|Q11498552|WD=Q11498552}}; {{ill|seven great temples founded by Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q11610998}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q339859]] Wikidata name: Shitennō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Ōsaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Arahakasan Shitennō-ji | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Shitennoji03s3200.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = {{nihongo||中心伽藍|[[shichidō garan|Chushin garan]]}} {{Infobox mapframe|type=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=220}} | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|39|14.04|N|135|30|59.22|E|region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | location = 1-1-18 Shitennō-ji | municipality = [[:en:Tennōji-ku, Osaka|Tennōji-ku]], Osaka | prefecture = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka-fu]] | religious_affiliation = [[Wa-shū]] | deity = [[Kannon]] (Avalokiteśvara) | country = Japan | functional_status = | general_contractor = [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] | established=593 | website = {{url|shitennoji.or.jp}} | founded_by = [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] | year_completed = 1963 (reconstruction) }}
'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
<gallery widths="240" heights="200"> Shitennô-ji Model.jpg|Model of the temple complex at the time of its construction, at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]]. Shitennō-ji main hall and pagoda.png|Tinted photo of the larger main hall and pagoda around 1880, prior to the downscaled modern reconstruction. Taken by [[:en:Kusakabe Kimbei|Kusakabe Kimbei]]. 1934 Typhoon Muroto damage at Shitenno-ji.jpg|The rubble of the five-tiered pagoda after destruction by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|1934 Muroto typhoon]]. </gallery>
== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The temple Prince Shōtoku built to honor them had four institutions, each to help the Japanese attain a higher level of civilization. This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. Three of the four sections are known to have existed inside the temple in Kamakura period{{Citation needed|date=March 2009}}.
The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. Surrounding this central complex are the {{nihongo|Great South Gate||Nandaimon}}, and a {{nihongo|Great East Gate||Higashi-no-ō'mon}}. To the west is the {{nihongo|Great West Gate||Nishi-no-ō'mon}}, also known as {{nihongo||極楽門|Gokuraku-mon}}. Further to the west is a stone [[torii]], which is imagined to be the Eastern Gate to [[:en:Sukhavati|Sukhavati]], the Pure Land of the West.
In the Kameido hall is a 7th-century turtle-shaped stonework that was used for state rituals with water.<ref name="mainichi">{{cite news |newspaper=Mainichi Daily News |title=Turtle-shaped stonework at Osaka temple dates to 7th century: study |date=April 27, 2019 |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124075101/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-date=November 24, 2020}}</ref> These are 2 turtle-shaped objects in opposite direction.<ref name="mainichi"/> The stone tank and upper turtle's base were carved from single pieces of Tatsuyama stone. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="5"> File:Shitennoji - pagoda.jpg|Shitennō-ji [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] File:Shitennoji07s3200.jpg|Kondō File:Shitennoji01s3200.jpg|The Great West Gate (Gokuraku-mon) File:Shitennoji daimon1.jpg|The Great East Gate File:Shitennoji daimon.jpg|The Great South Gate ({{transliteration|ja|Nandai-mon}}) File:Shitennoji4.jpg|Northern belfry File:Shitennoji-taishi-indoushoudo.jpg|Southern belfry File:Shitennoji-torii.jpg|Stone torii File:Shitennoji06s3200.jpg|Courtyard File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou1.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou2.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio1.jpg|Daimon 1 File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio2.jpg|Daimon 2 File:Shitennoji-KoboDaishi2 Osaka 20101013.jpg|Statuettes of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] </gallery>

'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>

== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The temple Prince Shōtoku built to honor them had four institutions, each to help the Japanese attain a higher level of civilization. This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. Three of the four sections are known to have existed inside the temple in Kamakura period{{Citation needed|date=March 2009}}.
The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. Surrounding this central complex are the {{nihongo|Great South Gate||Nandaimon}}, and a {{nihongo|Great East Gate||Higashi-no-ō'mon}}. To the west is the {{nihongo|Great West Gate||Nishi-no-ō'mon}}, also known as {{nihongo||極楽門|Gokuraku-mon}}. Further to the west is a stone [[torii]], which is imagined to be the Eastern Gate to [[:en:Sukhavati|Sukhavati]], the Pure Land of the West.
In the Kameido hall is a 7th-century turtle-shaped stonework that was used for state rituals with water.<ref name="mainichi">{{cite news |newspaper=Mainichi Daily News |title=Turtle-shaped stonework at Osaka temple dates to 7th century: study |date=April 27, 2019 |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124075101/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-date=November 24, 2020}}</ref> These are 2 turtle-shaped objects in opposite direction.<ref name="mainichi"/> The stone tank and upper turtle's base were carved from single pieces of Tatsuyama stone. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]

{{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|New Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q11504057}}; {{ill|Q11078809|WD=Q11078809}}; {{ill|Q11274431|WD=Q11274431}}; {{ill|Q11498552|WD=Q11498552}}; {{ill|Q11627443|WD=Q11627443}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|The Twenty-Fifth Reikyo of Dharan Shonin|WD=Q11555252}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Osaka|WD=Q11261255}}; {{ill|seven great temples founded by Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q11610998}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q339859]] Wikidata name: Shitennō-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Ōsaka, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Arahakasan Shitennō-ji | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Shitennoji03s3200.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright= | alt = | caption = {{nihongo||中心伽藍|[[shichidō garan|Chushin garan]]}} {{Infobox mapframe|type=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=220}} | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|39|14.04|N|135|30|59.22|E|region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | location = 1-1-18 Shitennō-ji | municipality = [[:en:Tennōji-ku, Osaka|Tennōji-ku]], Osaka | prefecture = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka-fu]] | religious_affiliation = [[Wa-shū]] | deity = [[Kannon]] (Avalokiteśvara) | country = Japan | functional_status = | general_contractor = [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] | established=593 | website = {{url|shitennoji.or.jp}} | founded_by = [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] | year_completed = 1963 (reconstruction) }}
'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
<gallery widths="240" heights="200"> Shitennô-ji Model.jpg|Model of the temple complex at the time of its construction, at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]]. Shitennō-ji main hall and pagoda.png|Tinted photo of the larger main hall and pagoda around 1880, prior to the downscaled modern reconstruction. Taken by [[:en:Kusakabe Kimbei|Kusakabe Kimbei]]. 1934 Typhoon Muroto damage at Shitenno-ji.jpg|The rubble of the five-tiered pagoda after destruction by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|1934 Muroto typhoon]]. </gallery>
== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The temple Prince Shōtoku built to honor them had four institutions, each to help the Japanese attain a higher level of civilization. This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. Three of the four sections are known to have existed inside the temple in Kamakura period{{Citation needed|date=March 2009}}.
The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. Surrounding this central complex are the {{nihongo|Great South Gate||Nandaimon}}, and a {{nihongo|Great East Gate||Higashi-no-ō'mon}}. To the west is the {{nihongo|Great West Gate||Nishi-no-ō'mon}}, also known as {{nihongo||極楽門|Gokuraku-mon}}. Further to the west is a stone [[torii]], which is imagined to be the Eastern Gate to [[:en:Sukhavati|Sukhavati]], the Pure Land of the West.
In the Kameido hall is a 7th-century turtle-shaped stonework that was used for state rituals with water.<ref name="mainichi">{{cite news |newspaper=Mainichi Daily News |title=Turtle-shaped stonework at Osaka temple dates to 7th century: study |date=April 27, 2019 |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124075101/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-date=November 24, 2020}}</ref> These are 2 turtle-shaped objects in opposite direction.<ref name="mainichi"/> The stone tank and upper turtle's base were carved from single pieces of Tatsuyama stone. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="5"> File:Shitennoji - pagoda.jpg|Shitennō-ji [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] File:Shitennoji07s3200.jpg|Kondō File:Shitennoji01s3200.jpg|The Great West Gate (Gokuraku-mon) File:Shitennoji daimon1.jpg|The Great East Gate File:Shitennoji daimon.jpg|The Great South Gate ({{transliteration|ja|Nandai-mon}}) File:Shitennoji4.jpg|Northern belfry File:Shitennoji-taishi-indoushoudo.jpg|Southern belfry File:Shitennoji-torii.jpg|Stone torii File:Shitennoji06s3200.jpg|Courtyard File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou1.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou2.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio1.jpg|Daimon 1 File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio2.jpg|Daimon 2 File:Shitennoji-KoboDaishi2 Osaka 20101013.jpg|Statuettes of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] </gallery>

'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
<gallery widths="240" heights="200"> Shitennô-ji Model.jpg|Model of the temple complex at the time of its construction, at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]]. Shitennō-ji main hall and pagoda.png|Tinted photo of the larger main hall and pagoda around 1880, prior to the downscaled modern reconstruction. Taken by [[:en:Kusakabe Kimbei|Kusakabe Kimbei]]. 1934 Typhoon Muroto damage at Shitenno-ji.jpg|The rubble of the five-tiered pagoda after destruction by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|1934 Muroto typhoon]]. </gallery>
== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The temple Prince Shōtoku built to honor them had four institutions, each to help the Japanese attain a higher level of civilization. This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. Three of the four sections are known to have existed inside the temple in Kamakura period{{Citation needed|date=March 2009}}.
The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. Surrounding this central complex are the {{nihongo|Great South Gate||Nandaimon}}, and a {{nihongo|Great East Gate||Higashi-no-ō'mon}}. To the west is the {{nihongo|Great West Gate||Nishi-no-ō'mon}}, also known as {{nihongo||極楽門|Gokuraku-mon}}. Further to the west is a stone [[torii]], which is imagined to be the Eastern Gate to [[:en:Sukhavati|Sukhavati]], the Pure Land of the West.
In the Kameido hall is a 7th-century turtle-shaped stonework that was used for state rituals with water.<ref name="mainichi">{{cite news |newspaper=Mainichi Daily News |title=Turtle-shaped stonework at Osaka temple dates to 7th century: study |date=April 27, 2019 |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124075101/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-date=November 24, 2020}}</ref> These are 2 turtle-shaped objects in opposite direction.<ref name="mainichi"/> The stone tank and upper turtle's base were carved from single pieces of Tatsuyama stone. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="5"> File:Shitennoji - pagoda.jpg|Shitennō-ji [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] File:Shitennoji07s3200.jpg|Kondō File:Shitennoji01s3200.jpg|The Great West Gate (Gokuraku-mon) File:Shitennoji daimon1.jpg|The Great East Gate File:Shitennoji daimon.jpg|The Great South Gate ({{transliteration|ja|Nandai-mon}}) File:Shitennoji4.jpg|Northern belfry File:Shitennoji-taishi-indoushoudo.jpg|Southern belfry File:Shitennoji-torii.jpg|Stone torii File:Shitennoji06s3200.jpg|Courtyard File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou1.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou2.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio1.jpg|Daimon 1 File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio2.jpg|Daimon 2 File:Shitennoji-KoboDaishi2 Osaka 20101013.jpg|Statuettes of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] </gallery>

'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>

One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>

This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]

|image_upright= | alt = | caption = {{nihongo||中心伽藍|[[shichidō garan|Chushin garan]]}} {{Infobox mapframe|type=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=220}} | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|39|14.04|N|135|30|59.22|E|region:JP_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | location = 1-1-18 Shitennō-ji | municipality = [[:en:Tennōji-ku, Osaka|Tennōji-ku]], Osaka | prefecture = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka-fu]] | religious_affiliation = [[Wa-shū]] | deity = [[Kannon]] (Avalokiteśvara) | country = Japan | functional_status = | general_contractor = [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] | established=593 | website = {{url|shitennoji.or.jp}} | founded_by = [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] | year_completed = 1963 (reconstruction) }}
'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
<gallery widths="240" heights="200"> Shitennô-ji Model.jpg|Model of the temple complex at the time of its construction, at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]]. Shitennō-ji main hall and pagoda.png|Tinted photo of the larger main hall and pagoda around 1880, prior to the downscaled modern reconstruction. Taken by [[:en:Kusakabe Kimbei|Kusakabe Kimbei]]. 1934 Typhoon Muroto damage at Shitenno-ji.jpg|The rubble of the five-tiered pagoda after destruction by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|1934 Muroto typhoon]]. </gallery>
== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The temple Prince Shōtoku built to honor them had four institutions, each to help the Japanese attain a higher level of civilization. This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. Three of the four sections are known to have existed inside the temple in Kamakura period{{Citation needed|date=March 2009}}.
The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. Surrounding this central complex are the {{nihongo|Great South Gate||Nandaimon}}, and a {{nihongo|Great East Gate||Higashi-no-ō'mon}}. To the west is the {{nihongo|Great West Gate||Nishi-no-ō'mon}}, also known as {{nihongo||極楽門|Gokuraku-mon}}. Further to the west is a stone [[torii]], which is imagined to be the Eastern Gate to [[:en:Sukhavati|Sukhavati]], the Pure Land of the West.
In the Kameido hall is a 7th-century turtle-shaped stonework that was used for state rituals with water.<ref name="mainichi">{{cite news |newspaper=Mainichi Daily News |title=Turtle-shaped stonework at Osaka temple dates to 7th century: study |date=April 27, 2019 |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124075101/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-date=November 24, 2020}}</ref> These are 2 turtle-shaped objects in opposite direction.<ref name="mainichi"/> The stone tank and upper turtle's base were carved from single pieces of Tatsuyama stone. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="5"> File:Shitennoji - pagoda.jpg|Shitennō-ji [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] File:Shitennoji07s3200.jpg|Kondō File:Shitennoji01s3200.jpg|The Great West Gate (Gokuraku-mon) File:Shitennoji daimon1.jpg|The Great East Gate File:Shitennoji daimon.jpg|The Great South Gate ({{transliteration|ja|Nandai-mon}}) File:Shitennoji4.jpg|Northern belfry File:Shitennoji-taishi-indoushoudo.jpg|Southern belfry File:Shitennoji-torii.jpg|Stone torii File:Shitennoji06s3200.jpg|Courtyard File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou1.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou2.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio1.jpg|Daimon 1 File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio2.jpg|Daimon 2 File:Shitennoji-KoboDaishi2 Osaka 20101013.jpg|Statuettes of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] </gallery>

prefecture = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka-fu]] | religious_affiliation = [[Wa-shū]] | deity = [[Kannon]] (Avalokiteśvara) | country = Japan | functional_status = | general_contractor = [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] | established=593 | website = {{url|shitennoji.or.jp}} | founded_by = [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] | year_completed = 1963 (reconstruction) }}
'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
<gallery widths="240" heights="200"> Shitennô-ji Model.jpg|Model of the temple complex at the time of its construction, at [[:en:Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum|Osaka Prefectural Chikatsu Asuka Museum]]. Shitennō-ji main hall and pagoda.png|Tinted photo of the larger main hall and pagoda around 1880, prior to the downscaled modern reconstruction. Taken by [[:en:Kusakabe Kimbei|Kusakabe Kimbei]]. 1934 Typhoon Muroto damage at Shitenno-ji.jpg|The rubble of the five-tiered pagoda after destruction by the [[:en:1934 Muroto typhoon|1934 Muroto typhoon]]. </gallery>
== Description == "[[Four Heavenly Kings|Shitennō]]" refer to the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] in Buddhism. The temple Prince Shōtoku built to honor them had four institutions, each to help the Japanese attain a higher level of civilization. This {{nihongo3|Four Institutions|四箇院|Shika-in}} was centered on the seven-building {{nihongo||伽藍|[[shichidō garan|garan]]}} (the complex inside the walls), and included a {{nihongo3|Institution of Religion and Education||Kyōden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|welfare Institution||Hiden-in}}, a {{nihongo3|hospital||Ryōbyō-in}}, and a {{nihongo3|pharmacy||Seiyaku-in}} to provide essential care to the people of Japan. Three of the four sections are known to have existed inside the temple in Kamakura period{{Citation needed|date=March 2009}}.
The {{transliteration|ja|garan}} consists of a five-story pagoda, a main Golden Pavilion ({{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Kondō]]}}) housing an image of the Bodhisattva Kannon, and a {{nihongo3|Lecture Hall||Kōdō}} under a covered corridor holding three gates; the {{nihongo|Deva Gate|仁王門|[[:en:Niōmon|Niōmon]]}} (also known as the {{nihongo||中門|chūmon}}), the Western Gate, and the Eastern Gate. Surrounding this central complex are the {{nihongo|Great South Gate||Nandaimon}}, and a {{nihongo|Great East Gate||Higashi-no-ō'mon}}. To the west is the {{nihongo|Great West Gate||Nishi-no-ō'mon}}, also known as {{nihongo||極楽門|Gokuraku-mon}}. Further to the west is a stone [[torii]], which is imagined to be the Eastern Gate to [[:en:Sukhavati|Sukhavati]], the Pure Land of the West.
In the Kameido hall is a 7th-century turtle-shaped stonework that was used for state rituals with water.<ref name="mainichi">{{cite news |newspaper=Mainichi Daily News |title=Turtle-shaped stonework at Osaka temple dates to 7th century: study |date=April 27, 2019 |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201124075101/https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20190427/p2a/00m/0na/015000c |archive-date=November 24, 2020}}</ref> These are 2 turtle-shaped objects in opposite direction.<ref name="mainichi"/> The stone tank and upper turtle's base were carved from single pieces of Tatsuyama stone. These are similar to the carving at the Sakafuneishi ruins which are believed to be a ritual site for [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]] (594-661) in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], Nara Prefecture.<ref name="mainichi"/> Nowadays they are still used for rituals to commemorate ancestors by floating sheets of wood with their names on the water.<ref name="mainichi"/>
Souvenirs of Shitennō-ji are sold on the 21st of each month.{{Citation needed|date=January 2021}}
== Directions == *a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station|Shitennoji-mae Yuhigaoka Station]] on the [[:en:Osaka Municipal Subway|Osaka Municipal Subway]] [[:en:Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Tennoji Station|Tennoji Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] Lines and the Osaka Municipal Subway Lines *a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Osaka Abenobashi Station|Osaka Abenobashi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|Minami-Osaka Line]]
==Gallery== <gallery perrow="5"> File:Shitennoji - pagoda.jpg|Shitennō-ji [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]] File:Shitennoji07s3200.jpg|Kondō File:Shitennoji01s3200.jpg|The Great West Gate (Gokuraku-mon) File:Shitennoji daimon1.jpg|The Great East Gate File:Shitennoji daimon.jpg|The Great South Gate ({{transliteration|ja|Nandai-mon}}) File:Shitennoji4.jpg|Northern belfry File:Shitennoji-taishi-indoushoudo.jpg|Southern belfry File:Shitennoji-torii.jpg|Stone torii File:Shitennoji06s3200.jpg|Courtyard File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou1.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitenno-ji Niomon zou2.jpg|Niōmon File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio1.jpg|Daimon 1 File:Shitennoji_daimon_nio2.jpg|Daimon 2 File:Shitennoji-KoboDaishi2 Osaka 20101013.jpg|Statuettes of [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]] </gallery>

'''Shitennō-ji''' ({{langx|ja|四天王寺}}, ''Temple of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]'') is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]], Japan. It is also known as '''Arahaka-ji''', '''Nanba-ji''', or '''Mitsu-ji'''. The temple is sometimes regarded as the first Buddhist and oldest officially administered temple in Japan,<ref name="scheid">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/anm_torii.htm#aussershinto|title=Religion in Japan|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|work=Torii|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|accessdate=12 February 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.art-and-archaeology.com/japan/asukadera1.html|title=Asuka-Dera Temple|accessdate=29 September 2016}}</ref> although the temple complex and buildings have been rebuilt over the centuries, with the last reconstruction taking place in 1963. Shortly after World War II, Shitennō-ji became independent of the parent [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect and formed the Wa sect (''wa-shū'', {{lang|ja|和宗}}) of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite web|title=四天王寺の歴史, 和宗総本山 四天王寺| language=ja| url=https://www.shitennoji.or.jp/history.html| accessdate = 2023-09-15}}</ref>
== History == [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shōtoku]] was known for his profound Buddhist faith when [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] was not widespread in Japan during the 6th century.<ref name="nikkei"/> In order to popularize Buddhism, Prince Shōtoku led a massive national project to promote Buddhism and he commissioned the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei"/> Prince Shōtoku invited three Korean carpenters from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]].<ref name="nikkei"/> They brought knowledge and led the construction of Shitennō-ji.<ref name="nikkei">{{cite web |website=Nikkei |title=Japan's oldest company defies time with merit-based succession |author=Yasuhiko Nakazawa |date=December 31, 2020 |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210104075413/https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Companies/Japan-s-oldest-company-defies-time-with-merit-based-succession |archive-date=January 4, 2021}}</ref> The commission of Shitennō-ji was part of a massive national project led by Prince Shōtoku.<ref name="nikkei"/>
The temple buildings themselves have been rebuilt a few times over the centuries; most of the present structures are from when the temple was last completely rebuilt in 1963. One of the members involved in the initial construction of the temple in the 6th century later established the firm [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]], which specialized in temple and shrine buildings. [[:en:Kongō Gumi|Kongō Gumi]] was the world's [[:en:List of oldest companies|oldest company]] until it was acquired by the [[:en:Takamatsu Construction Group|Takamatsu Construction Group]] in 2004.<ref name="bloomberg">{{cite web |title=The End of a 1,400-Year-Old Business |website=Bloomberg |url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |date=April 17, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311042613/http://www.bloomberg.com:80/news/articles/2007-04-16/the-end-of-a-1-400-year-old-businessbusinessweek-business-news-stock-market-and-financial-advice |archive-date=March 11, 2016}}</ref>
